candidates.name: Build Clinic
candidates.summary: A recurring session where participants bring one real task and leave with a working automation. The goal is a concrete time-saving tool produced in the session, not a demo.
mechanics: Participants nominate a recurring task at least 24 hours before the session.
A facilitator or peer mentor helps scope the task to a single-session build.
Each participant builds their own automation using no-code or AI tools (Zapier, Make, n8n, Claude, etc.).
Session ends with a live test of the tool and a brief retro on what to refine.
Working automations are posted to a shared prompt/recipe library for team reuse.
proof: One working automation per participant per session.
Automation saves measurable recurring time within one week of the clinic.

known_uses.name: AI Workshop — individual hands-on automation build
known_uses.quote: Every person on your team leaves with a working AI system in their specific workflow—not a group demo, but individual builds.
known_uses.note: Half-day format; same-day working system per participant.

known_uses.name: Soraia AI & Automation Workshop
known_uses.quote: The hands-on experience was incredibly practical and useful, providing real solutions to business needs.
known_uses.note: 4-hour hybrid workshop (1h theory + 3h hands-on), multilingual (IT/ES/FR/EN).

known_uses.name: Vision Base 生成AI・AIエージェント 2日間集中セミナー
known_uses.quote: 自社業務を即日プロトタイピング
known_uses.note: Japanese enterprise: same-day prototyping of company operations; 2-day intensive, Tokyo. lang: ja

candidates.name: Build Sprint
aliases: AIエージェントハッカソン
candidates.summary: A time-boxed intensive (48–72 hours, or a structured 5-day sprint) where mixed teams build and ship a working automation or tool by the end. Mentors float to unblock; the output is a live tool plus shared prompts, not a slide deck.
mechanics: Pre-assign teams of 4–5 by job function; include at least one technical participant per team.
Set clear problem space before the event: identify automation gaps in current workflows.
Use alternating workshop and build days (e.g. Workshop Wednesday's Mon/Wed/Fri + Tue/Thu build rhythm).
Provide API keys, compute credits, and starter templates to remove setup friction.
Mentor-to-team ratio of 1 pair per 2–3 teams.
Demo day: each team presents a working tool; judges evaluate on practical business value over technical complexity.
Winners' prompts and automation blueprints go into the shared library.
proof: Each team ships a deployed, working automation by the final day.
Post-event: at least one tool is in active use the following week.
Prompt blueprints from winning solutions are added to the team's shared library.

known_uses.quote: 40-50 employees from the country's largest music licensing company (STIM) gathered for an impossible challenge: create functional AI solutions in just two days. By the end of 48 hours, they had built three working AI systems.
known_uses.note: Detailed enterprise playbook; mentor ratio, team sizing, budget, evaluation framework (Three I's: Idea, Implementation, Impact).

known_uses.name: Workshop Wednesday — AI Sprint: Ship in One Week
known_uses.quote: Every sprint ends with an automation your team runs next Monday.
known_uses.note: 5-day alternating workshop/build format; deliverables: deployed automation + 100-day rollout plan.

known_uses.name: Domo Enterprise AI Hackathon — Workflows
known_uses.quote: Building enterprise AI through hackathons delivers working workflows across customer service, marketing, product design, and sales in just 48 hours.
known_uses.note: SODA framework: Standardize → Optimize → Digitize → Automate.

known_uses.name: AngelHack AI Hackathon Guide for Teams
known_uses.quote: The quality of your submissions depends partly on how well you prepare participants before they start building.
known_uses.note: Covers virtual (1–4 weeks) and in-person (2-day) formats; judging criteria: technical execution, innovation, impact, presentation.

candidates.name: Shared Move Library
candidates.summary: A team-maintained, searchable collection of proven prompts and automation blueprints that anyone can reuse and improve. It democratizes AI expertise and locks in gains from Build Clinics and Build Sprints.
mechanics: Choose a shared platform with search, folder categories, and role-based permissions (e.g. Notion, SharePoint, TeamAI, PromptFluent).
Organize entries by role, task type, or project; apply a strict naming convention.
Gate edits behind a review role so quality stays high, but allow any team member to view and use.
After every Build Clinic or Sprint, winning prompts are added within 24 hours.
Version-track prompts; mark outdated ones; remove or archive after a review cycle.
Run an optional recurring 'Prompt Friday' (15 min) where someone demos a new or improved entry.
proof: Library contains at least 20 reviewed, production-tested prompts within 90 days of launch.
Team members cite the library as source of at least one reused automation per sprint.

known_uses.name: Wharton Generative AI Labs Prompt Library
known_uses.quote: Prompting with LLMs always begins with a goal in mind. What do you want to accomplish? What does success look like?
known_uses.note: CC BY 4.0 licensed; evidence-based templates with instructions, use cases, model options. Widely referenced as enterprise starting point.

known_uses.name: TeamAI Shared Prompt Libraries
known_uses.quote: A library gives you more control over quality because you decide which inputs are added and set rules for details like style and tone.
known_uses.note: Platform with role-based permissions; department-specific libraries and company-wide shared sets.

known_uses.name: Kartaca Corporate Prompt Library
known_uses.quote: Shared Drives ensure that prompts belong to the team, not an individual.
known_uses.note: RCTC framework for reusable prompt templates; governance via naming conventions and iteration-as-policy.

candidates.name: No-Code Workflow Track
candidates.summary: A structured, self-paced course track (2–8 hours) that teaches a non-technical person to build their first real automation using a no-code workflow tool. Ends with a badge or certification proving basic automation competency.
mechanics: Learner navigates a platform (Zapier, Make, n8n) guided by text or video tutorials.
Each module introduces one concept and immediately builds a small workflow.
Final workflow integrates at least 3 real apps and performs a useful business task.
Learner takes a knowledge quiz (typically 80%+ pass threshold) to earn a badge.
Badge is shareable on LinkedIn or in email signatures.
proof: Learner earns a platform certification badge.
Completed workflow runs successfully on a live schedule or trigger.

candidates.name: Automation Sprint Bootcamp
candidates.summary: A part-time, multi-week bootcamp (4–12 weeks) that takes a non-engineer from zero to building and deploying AI-powered automations. Structured as sprints with real business deliverables; ends with a capstone project.
mechanics: Program divided into 2-week sprints; each sprint has a hands-on project tied to a real use case.
Covers: prompt engineering, LLM fundamentals, workflow automation tools (Zapier/Make/n8n), chatbot building, API/webhook integrations, and AI agent basics.
Learner builds 5–7 progressively complex projects; final capstone is a solution to a real business problem.
Optional: peer cohort with live tutoring sessions and group reviews.
Capstone is reviewed and graded; certificate issued on completion.
proof: Capstone project: a deployed AI automation addressing a real workflow problem.
Certificate of completion with skills portfolio.

candidates.name: Build-Along
candidates.summary: A live or cohort-based course where non-engineers ship a small, real application using AI coding tools (Cursor, Lovable, Replit, Claude Code). The session moves at app-building speed, not lecture speed — every module ends with running code.
mechanics: Instructor builds a feature live; participants replicate it in their own project.
Tools: Lovable, Replit, Cursor, Claude Code, v0 or similar AI-first builders.
Each week: one new capability added to the same running app.
Prompting discipline is taught alongside tool use: how to describe what you want, how to debug output.
Capstone: a personal flagship app with documentation, version history, and prompt notes.
Community channel for async unblocking between sessions.
proof: Participant ships and deploys a working web application by week 8.
Application is publicly accessible and demoed at a final session.

candidates.name: Task Automation Reskilling Sprint
candidates.summary: A structured multi-week program that moves operations or business staff from manual repetitive work to AI-automated workflows. Focused on identify-automate-deploy cycles using RPA, AI agents, and no-code tools; no coding required.
mechanics: Sprint 1: Identify and map repetitive tasks; calculate current time cost per task.
Sprint 2–3: Build AI-powered chatbots, workflows, and simple integrations.
Sprint 4: Introduce RPA for processes that cannot be handled by no-code tools alone.
Sprint 5+: Advanced integrations (APIs, webhooks); build AI agents for decision tasks.
Capstone: One complete automation solution addressing a real departmental problem.
Career/role transition support included (portfolio, job prep).
proof: Capstone: deployed automation reducing identifiable manual work hours per week.
Completion certificate.

candidates.name: Anti-pattern: Vibe-Ship Without Review
candidates.summary: Shipping AI-generated code without any human review or testing cycle. The speed of vibe-coding tools tempts makers to deploy code they cannot read or verify, creating security, correctness, and maintenance blind spots.
mechanics: Symptom: participant ships multiple apps per day without reading a single line of generated code.
Symptom: production app uses leaked API keys or unchecked user-supplied inputs.
Counter-move: require a minimum-viable review step (read the diff, run a test, check the prompt notes) before any deploy.
Counter-move: add a 'discernment check' Threshold to the Build-Along track: learner must explain what two generated functions do before advancing.
proof: Removed from being an anti-pattern when the build-along track formally includes a discernment checkpoint.

sources.title: Vibe Coding Explained — Google Cloud
sources.quote: vibe coding offers intoxicating generative speed at the cost of nearly all technical control

candidates.name: Checkpoint-Gated Spike
candidates.summary: A strictly time-boxed (typically 1-3 day) experiment whose deliverable is knowledge, not shipped code: when facing an unknown — e.g. whether an LLM can do a task reliably — the team runs a small experiment with a defined question and a checkpoint at the end of the box, rather than speculating or open-ended researching. It pays off by converting uncertainty into a decision while capping the investment.
mechanics: Name the specific question, risk, or uncertainty to resolve
Set a hard time box (commonly 1-3 days) and a fixed budget
Run a small, almost throwaway experiment — prototype, probe, or research
At the time-box checkpoint, present findings: decision, proof of concept, or estimate
Either proceed with confidence or split the question into smaller spikes
Capture the learning so it informs the real story (the spike output is knowledge)
proof: SAFe defines the deliverable as risk reduction: gaining the knowledge necessary to reduce the risk of a technical approach or increase estimate reliability.
Mountain Goat: because spikes are time-boxed, the investment is fixed — so a failed exploration costs a bounded, known amount.

known_uses.name: Spikes (Scaled Agile Framework)
known_uses.quote: When faced with a question, risk, or uncertainty, Agile Teams conduct small experiments before moving to implementation rather than speculate about the outcome.

known_uses.name: Spikes (Mountain Goat Software / Mike Cohn)
known_uses.quote: With a spike, a team isn't trying to immediately deliver a new capability; instead, they are building the knowledge that will allow them to deliver the new capability later.

sources.title: Spikes - Scaled Agile Framework
sources.quote: Spikes represent activities such as exploration, architecture, infrastructure, research, design, and prototyping.

sources.title: What Are Agile Spikes? When and Why Do Teams Use Spikes?
sources.quote: In agile projects, a spike refers to a time-boxed research activity that helps teams make better decisions & deliver better products.

candidates.rejected_reason: Superseded by the generic pedagogy pattern `experimental-exploration-with-checkpoints` authored in training-src/pedagogy-experiential.json; the SAFe enterprise-spike evidence was folded into that pattern's known_uses.
